data.title: Selective Insurance expects 14% operating ROE in 2026 while expanding technology investment
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Selective Insurance Group, Inc. (SIGI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO John J. Marchioni highlighted that "in 2025, we delivered an ROE of 14.4% and an operating ROE of 14.2%." He noted book value per share growth of 18% and $182 million returned to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. Marchioni stated, "We are well positioned to build on recent momentum," emphasizing ongoing efforts to improve underwriting profitability and margin improvement. He added, "We delivered a 93.8% combined ratio in the quarter, reducing our full year combined ratio to 97.2%, just outside the 96% to 97% guidance we provided at the beginning of the year and at the low end of the 97% to 98% guidance provided last quarter." Strategic efforts include expanding the geographic footprint, broadening E&S distribution capabilities, and tightening underwriting guidelines, especially in commercial auto and general liability.

  • CFO Patrick Brennan reported, "For the quarter, fully diluted EPS was $2.52, up 66% from a year ago. Non-GAAP operating EPS was $2.57, up 59%. Our return on equity was 18.3%, and our non-GAAP operating return on equity was 18.7%, reflecting continued strong investment performance." He also stated, "The GAAP combined ratio was 93.8%, a 4.7 point improvement from fourth quarter 2024, mainly because this quarter had no net prior year reserve development."

Outlook

  • The company expects a GAAP combined ratio between 96.5% and 97.5% for 2026, with guidance assuming 6 points of catastrophe losses. After-tax net investment income for 2026 is expected to be $465 million. Marchioni confirmed, "Our 2026 guidance implies an underlying combined ratio in the 90.5% to 91.5% range compared to the 91.8% we reported in 2025." The company targets an operating ROE in the 14% range for 2026 and expects the expense ratio to increase by about 0.5 point, driven by strategic technology investments.

Financial Results

  • Book value per share increased 18% in 2025. The company reported $3.6 billion in both GAAP equity and statutory surplus at year-end. Personal Lines combined ratio for the quarter was 103%, impacted by higher catastrophe losses and increased casualty loss costs, while the annual combined ratio for Personal Lines improved to 100.6% from 109.3% in 2024. E&S combined ratio for the quarter was 93.1% and 87.8% for the year. Net premiums written grew 5% year-over-year, largely due to deliberate actions to improve underwriting profitability. The company executed $86 million in share repurchases for the year, with $170 million remaining on authorization.

Q&A

  • Michael Phillips, Oppenheimer: Asked about confidence in improving commercial line margins given price deceleration and elevated casualty loss picks. Marchioni responded, "We're confident in the guidance we're providing you... that underlying combined ratio improvement of 80 basis points, if you just focus on the midpoint of our underlying combined ratio, we think, is reasonable."
  • Phillips followed up on changes in setting case reserves for commercial auto and GL. Marchioni explained, "We've seen movement from an incurred basis similar to what we've seen on the paid side... We're looking at paid and incurred methods. We're projecting that to ultimate."
  • Jon Paul Newsome, Piper Sandler: Asked for details on reserve development in Personal Lines. Marchioni clarified, "In personal auto, the prior year development is driven entirely by the state of New Jersey... all of it is the 2024 accident year."
  • Jing Li, KBW: Queried E&S casualty reserves and impact on growth appetite. Marchioni stated the reserve action was "$10 million, so de minimis in total, but spread across the 2020 through 2023 accident years."
  • Rowland Mayor, RBC: Asked about workers' comp releases and accident years. Marchioni explained half of the favorable emergence was from the annual tail study, the rest from accident years 2022 and prior.
  • Michael Zaremski, BMO: Inquired about the underlying loss ratio and expense ratio guidance. Marchioni confirmed stable severity assumptions and noted, "We expect the investment in technology to continue to rise as a percentage of premium and the cost of labor... to be coming down over time."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed on reserve adequacy, commercial auto trends, and the impact of technology spend, with a tone that was neutral to slightly skeptical, seeking more detail on reserving and growth. Questions often probed for clarity on margin improvement and risk mitigation.
  • Management was confident and measured, consistently emphasizing discipline and strategic execution. Marchioni frequently reiterated confidence in guidance and reserve processes, stating, "We're confident in the guidance we're providing you." Compared to the previous quarter, the tone showed increased emphasis on operational discipline and technology investments.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company shifted its combined ratio guidance range from 97% to 98% last quarter to 96.5% to 97.5% for 2026. Management tone became more assertive about technology-driven efficiency and underwriting actions, whereas the previous quarter focused more on reserve adequacy and third-party reviews. Analysts' focus remained steady on reserve trends and margin improvement but pressed more on technology investment and its expected returns this quarter. Key metric changes included significant improvement in the GAAP combined ratio and an explicit increase in technology investment as a strategic priority.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing challenges in commercial auto, particularly New Jersey, and acknowledged elevated loss trends in casualty lines. Marchioni stated, "We are addressing commercial auto with both underwriting and claims actions," including tighter guidelines and focused telematics rollout. The regulatory environment in New Jersey was described as a continuing challenge. Technology investment and its impact on the expense ratio were highlighted as necessary for long-term efficiency but recognized as a short-term margin pressure.

Final Takeaway

Selective Insurance Group closed 2025 with strong improvements in profitability, robust investment income, and clear actions to address underwriting profitability—particularly in commercial auto and New Jersey personal auto. The company projects continued margin improvement in 2026, supported by disciplined underwriting, technology investments, and geographic expansion, while maintaining a confident outlook on achieving a 14% operating ROE and delivering long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Invesco Mortgage Capital signals continued leverage stability as book value rises 4.5% amid agency MBS strength
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Invesco Mortgage Capital Inc. (IVR) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO John M. Anzalone opened by noting that "financial conditions improved during the quarter, supported by 2 Federal Reserve rate cuts, solid corporate earnings, improved financial conditions and strong economic growth." He highlighted that equity markets extended gains and agency mortgages outperformed treasuries, aided by lower rate volatility and a supportive supply and demand environment.
  • Anzalone reported a 3.7% increase in book value per common share to $8.72 and combined with the recently increased dividend of $0.36 resulted in an 8% economic return for the quarter. He stated, "We modestly increased leverage to 7x, consistent with the constructive investment environment. At year-end, our $6.3 billion portfolio included $5.4 billion in Agency mortgages, $900 million in Agency CMBS and our liquidity position remained robust with $453 million in unrestricted cash and unencumbered assets."
  • Anzalone expressed a positive stance toward agency mortgages: "We remain positive on agency mortgages following the sharp decline in volatility, though we view near-term risks as balanced given recent strong performance and the announcement of $200 billion in agency mortgage purchases by Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac."
  • Chief Investment Officer Brian Norris explained, "The steeper yield curve benefits longer-term investments such as Agency RMBS and Agency CMBS and is supportive of our strategy." He noted a "significant decline in interest rate volatility since April, which provided a tailwind for risk assets, including Agency MBS in the second half of the year."
  • Norris revealed, "IVR's economic return of 8% during the fourth quarter is a result of that positive momentum, which has continued into 2026 with book value up approximately 4.5% since year-end through Wednesday of this week."

Outlook

  • Management described the environment as more normalized and supportive for agency mortgages, with the conclusion of quantitative tightening and the announcement of T-Bill purchases helping to solidify funding markets and tighten repo spreads. Norris stated, "While agency mortgage valuations have improved significantly over the past year, we believe the current environment is reflective of a more normalized investment landscape that continues to provide investors with attractive levered returns."
  • The company views near-term risks as balanced following recent outperformance, and expects the "current environment of low volatility in interest rates and spreads, along with further steepening of the yield curve and supportive supply and demand technicals will provide a positive backdrop for agency mortgages over the long term."

Financial Results

  • Book value per common share increased 3.7% to $8.72, and the economic return for the quarter was 8%.
  • The portfolio grew to $6.3 billion, including $5.4 billion in Agency mortgages and $900 million in Agency CMBS.
  • Liquidity position at year-end was $453 million in unrestricted cash and unencumbered assets.
  • Norris said, "Repurchase agreements collateralized by our Agency RMBS and Agency CMBS investments increased from $5.2 billion to $5.6 billion, consistent with the increase in our total assets, while the total notional of our hedges increased from $4.4 billion to $4.9 billion."
  • Leverage was modestly increased to 7x, and the company reported a higher percentage of pools valued at premium dollar prices.

Q&A

  • Trevor Cranston, Citizens JMP: Asked about leverage level and value within the coupon stack after the GSE buying announcement. Norris responded that leverage was increased in Q4, and "we're still pretty comfortable because the environment overall, even though spreads are tighter, it's pretty supportive with limited spread volatility." He added the company is finding value in "the belly, so call it 3.5 through 5.5s."
  • Cranston followed up on prepayment surprises in higher coupons. Norris indicated no surprises, noting, "We certainly saw an increase over the second half of the year in higher coupons in our 6s and 6.5s, prepayment speeds did increase. But because we do own prepaid protection, they certainly were less impacted than what you would see in generic collateral."
  • Jason Weaver, JonesTrading: Asked about rotation down in coupon and trade-offs between prepay risk and positioning. Norris responded, "To the extent that, that impacts higher coupons, I think the goal is likely to not necessarily reduce the allocation by selling, but to future purchases come a little bit lower in the coupon stack."
  • Weaver also inquired about updated estimated book value. Norris replied, "we were up about 4.5% through Wednesday."
  • Douglas Harter, UBS: Asked about capital structure and ability to raise capital. Anzalone said, "we do selectively access the ATM when the common stock provides clear benefits to shareholders."
  • Jason Stewart, Compass Point: Asked about the decision-making on the ATM and investment environment. Anzalone stated, "it is a combination of things when we make a decision on whether to issue...price to book is important...and it's other accretive investment opportunities."
  • Stewart asked about the impact of government intervention. Norris replied, "if we were to see that pace [of GSE purchases] continue to increase, that would be a pretty clear signal that at some point...plan to increase those caps."
  • Eric Hagen, BTIG: Asked about book value sensitivity to spread tightening. Norris responded, "the magnitude of the change in book value to spread changes is the same...our expectation for further spread tightening is significantly reduced."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on leverage, capital deployment, coupon stack strategy, capital structure, and sensitivity to spread movements; the tone was probing but not overtly negative, seeking clarity on risk and capital decisions.
  • Management maintained a constructive and confident tone in prepared remarks, using phrases such as "the environment overall...is pretty supportive" and "we remain positive on agency mortgages." In Q&A, the tone remained measured, with Norris acknowledging balanced risks and reduced expectations for further spread tightening, but reiterating comfort with current positioning.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was slightly more confident about the investment environment, while analysts maintained a similar level of inquiry around risk, capital, and positioning.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from a focus on broad-based policy easing and volatility decline to an emphasis on normalized investment conditions and balanced near-term risks.
  • Strategic focus evolved to highlight the impact of GSE purchases, the end of quantitative tightening, and an increased comfort with modestly higher leverage.
  • Analysts continued to focus on capital allocation, leverage, and risk-return, with greater attention this quarter to the effects of GSE actions and capital market conditions.
  • Book value per common share increased from $8.41 in Q3 to $8.72 in Q4, with economic return at 8% versus 8.7% in the prior quarter.
  • Management’s confidence level remained steady, with a greater willingness to increase leverage and deploy capital, while acknowledging that further spread tightening is less likely.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that near-term risks are balanced following strong recent performance and that further spread tightening is not expected unless there are significant changes in GSE caps or increased bank participation.
  • Norris cited, "our expectation for further spread tightening is significantly reduced...unless we start to see banks come in, in greater size and also increased caps."
  • Analysts raised concerns around capital structure, leverage, prepayment risk, and the sustainability of returns amid tighter spreads and GSE market interventions.

Final Takeaway

Invesco Mortgage Capital closed 2025 with improved book value, a robust liquidity position, and increased leverage in response to a supportive investment environment. Management remains constructive on agency mortgages, citing the benefits of reduced volatility, the conclusion of quantitative tightening, and GSE purchase programs, while viewing near-term risks as balanced and maintaining a disciplined approach to capital deployment and portfolio management.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: BayFirst signals ongoing SBA loan runoff and targets enhanced profitability with 2026 strategic plan
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BayFirst Financial Corp. (BAFN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Thomas Zernick stated that "the end of 2025 marks the completion of several significant milestones" as BayFirst executed and completed "a number of strategic initiatives, including the exit from the SBA 7(a) lending business, the sale of a substantial amount of 7(a) loan balances, a significant reduction in head count and expenses and a complete focus on our community bank." He emphasized that these efforts resulted in lower risk, more efficient operations, and a better position for sustainable growth.
  • Zernick highlighted that "the bank organically grew deposits $12.5 million in the fourth quarter and 85% of our deposits are insured," with a stable net interest margin at 3.58% and treasury management revenues showing a 69% improvement compared to Q4 2024.
  • The CEO acknowledged challenges with the legacy SBA 7(a) portfolio, noting "net charge-offs from unguaranteed SBA 7(a) loans were elevated," and additional charge-offs are likely, but expected to lessen over time. He stressed, "outside of the legacy SBA 7(a) business, community bank metrics look strong."
  • Zernick added, "the company's liquidity ratio was over 18% at year-end," and that this liquidity will support efforts to reduce high-cost deposits and enhance net interest margin.
  • CFO Scott McKim reported, "we are reporting a net loss of $2.5 million in the fourth quarter. This compares to the net loss of $18.9 million we reported in the third quarter, which also included a restructuring charge of $7.3 million and additional provision expense of $8.1 million."
  • McKim stated, "deposits increased $12.5 million or 1.1% during the fourth quarter of 2025 and increased $40.7 million or 3.6% over the past year to end at $1.18 billion."
  • President and COO Robin Oliver emphasized, "total nonperforming loans, excluding government-guaranteed balances were $16.9 million at the end of the fourth quarter, relatively flat to $16.5 million at the end of the third quarter." Oliver highlighted strengthened credit administration practices and aggressive management of problem credits.

Outlook

  • Zernick reiterated the company's "focus on what matters most, being the premier community bank in Tampa Bay." He stated that the 2026 strategic plan centers on "fortifying the balance sheet and focusing on maintaining a culture of disciplined risk management." He added, "we are confident in this plan and in our ability to execute it, and these priorities will enable us to flourish as a community bank and drive sustainable revenue growth."
  • Management expects ongoing runoff and potential sales of remaining SBA unguaranteed balances, with continuing efforts to bring classified assets in line with peers and reduce nonperforming and classified credits through 2026.
  • The company anticipates improvements in asset quality as the SBA portfolio winds down, expecting a lessening impact from additional charge-offs over time.

Financial Results

  • McKim reported net interest income was $11.2 million in the fourth quarter, and net interest margin was 3.58%, down 3 basis points from the third quarter.
  • Loans held for investment decreased by $34.8 million during the quarter to end at $963.9 million, while loans held for sale decreased by $94.1 million, reflecting the Banesco USA transaction.
  • Noninterest income was a negative $104,000 for the quarter, which McKim noted was $900,000 better than Q3, but a decrease from $22.3 million in Q4 2024 due to the exit from SBA 7(a) lending and the absence of gain on sale revenue.
  • Noninterest expense was $11.9 million for the quarter, a decrease of $13.3 million compared to Q3, driven primarily by the absence of the restructuring charge.
  • Provision expense for credit losses was $2 million, and net charge-offs were $4.6 million, with unguaranteed SBA 7(a) loans accounting for $4.1 million of the total.

Q&A

  • Ross Haberman, RLH Investments, LLC: "Could you focus on the -- I think you said it was $171 million of unguaranteed government loans portion. What is the specific allowance against that? And what's been your recent default experience on that category?" Scott McKim: "That portfolio has against it about 13% of the balances are within our allowance for credit losses. So the default rate is much lower than that."
  • Haberman: "I thought a good portion of that had gone away with the loan sale. What is that -- what was that number -- what is that [indiscernible] I suppose?" McKim: "It was about $50.5 million higher at the end of the third quarter."
  • Julienne Cassarino, Sycamore Analytics LLC: "I was wondering if you could talk about the deposits... it looks like your deposit costs came down nicely... and if those are coming in, in part from the treasury management platform?" McKim: "They're doing that by growing and nurturing relationships with their customers... As we move forward and exit out of the SBA 7(a) business... we're being much more disciplined around it." Oliver added, "our treasury team was very busy and continues to be very busy... we're striking that balance, but that's going to be our continued focus in 2026 is to bring down any of those promotional rates, reduce any reliance on broker deposits, et cetera, to help reduce that cost of funds."
  • Cassarino: "How does the deposit franchise break down roughly between commercial deposits and retail?" McKim: "It is a very granular portfolio... we're very focused on growing our business relationships and making sure that as we establish new lending relationships that there's compensating deposits."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on risk, deposit growth, and the granularity of the deposit base, with questions seeking clarity on the legacy SBA loan portfolio and deposit cost trends.
  • Management's tone was measured and confident during prepared remarks, with Zernick acknowledging, "these results... fall short of our expectations, but we understand the responsibility we have to our shareholders to address these challenges and deliver better results." During the Q&A, management was detailed and transparent, offering specific figures and explanations without deflection.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts showed less skepticism and more interest in the mechanics of the bank's transition, while management maintained a consistent tone of accountability and forward-looking confidence.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company completed its exit from the SBA 7(a) lending business in Q4, which was a transition first announced and partly executed in Q3, with further sales of loan balances and ongoing runoff of the remaining portfolio.
  • Net loss narrowed from $18.9 million in Q3 (which included a $7.3 million restructuring charge and $8.1 million provision expense) to $2.5 million in Q4.
  • Deposits grew by $12.5 million in Q4 versus $7.7 million in Q3, with an increased focus on organic growth and lowering cost of funds.
  • The provision for credit losses decreased significantly quarter-over-quarter.
  • Management's tone remained focused on execution and risk management, with increased visibility on strategic direction and explicit acknowledgment of the responsibility to shareholders.
  • Analysts in the current quarter focused more on understanding the mechanics of the bank's new direction than expressing concern over execution risk, showing a shift in sentiment.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified the legacy SBA 7(a) loan portfolio as a continued source of risk, with Zernick and McKim both noting that additional charge-offs are likely, though the impact is expected to lessen.
  • The bank's efforts to reduce high-cost deposits and reliance on promotional rates could affect deposit growth if not carefully managed, as discussed by Oliver.
  • Credit risk management remains a significant area of focus, with management increasing resources and taking a conservative approach to resolving nonperforming loans.
  • Analyst questions targeted the adequacy of allowances for credit losses and the pace of runoff in the SBA portfolio, indicating ongoing concern over asset quality.

Final Takeaway

Coming out of Q4 2025, BayFirst Financial Corp. emphasized its strategic transition to a focused community banking model anchored in the Tampa Bay market, having executed a full exit from the SBA 7(a) business and reduced associated risks. Management highlighted improved deposit growth, stable net interest margin, and robust credit administration, while acknowledging ongoing risks with the legacy SBA portfolio and committing to further reductions in classified assets. The leadership team remains confident in achieving the objectives of its 2026 strategic plan, prioritizing balance sheet strength, disciplined risk management, and sustainable revenue growth as BayFirst seeks to enhance long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Missouri sports betting volume dazzles in the first month of action
data.description:
Missouri generated massive sports betting volume in December, according to a report from the Missouri Gaming Commission. The total handle during the month was $543.0M, which consisted of $4.1M at retail locations and $538.9M through mobile apps.

In terms of mobile betting, FanDuel (FLUT) led the state with $212.7M in total wagers. DraftKings (DKNG) generated $195.3M in betting action, followed by Bet365 with $57.8M and BetMGM (MGM) (GMVHF) with $28.1M. Fanatics (FANA) impressed with $22M in betting action despite having a lower profile. Caesars Sportsbook (CZR) was the only other app to generate more than $10M in betting action, with a tally of $14.5M. 

The $543M handle for Missouri is considered by analysts a strong start for a state that only ranks 19th in population. Notably, the sports betting total was more than the combined total for neighboring states Iowa and Kansas for December.

The online sportsbook operators were aggressive with promotions during the month, which means that the actual hold rate may be low amid the battle for market share.

Sports betting officially launched in Missouri on December 1.

data.title: The Mag 7 are no longer trading as a unified group – T. Rowe Price’s Tony Wang
data.description:
The “Magnificent Seven” tech companies (MAGS)—Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), and Nvidia (NVDA)—are no longer trading as a unified group, with investors now needing to evaluate each stock on its individual merits, according to Tony Wang, T. Rowe Price portfolio manager.

Wang described the current AI landscape as a “space race” that is “going to multiple moons,” with companies like Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Nvidia (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA) all pursuing distinct strategies and commanding increasingly different valuations.

In an interview with CNBC, Wang explained the contrasting market reactions to AI spending from Meta (META) and Microsoft (MSFT). While Meta surged on heavy AI investment plans, Microsoft declined on similar announcements.

“The market knew its cost but didn’t know its revenue,” Wang said of Meta’s (META) strong quarter, noting that when the company guided revenue growth from the low 20s to over 30%, “you can kind of spend whatever you want, because people believe that you’re getting a lot of ROI on it.”

Wang highlighted the diverse strengths across the tech landscape, pointing to Apple’s (AAPL) “great ecosystem on personal computing,” Meta’s (META) ability to roll out AI across its digital properties, and Tesla’s (TSLA) progress in full self-driving technology.

Meanwhile, Nvidia (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO) continue building out the AI infrastructure that underpins the entire sector’s growth.

The portfolio manager expressed particular excitement about agentic AI, citing tools like Claude as evidence of rapid advancement. “These are really big step changes that we’re seeing by the month,” Wang said, adding that this evolution ensures the “need for compute is going to continue to be there” as the technology matures.

Wang sees large-cap companies as best positioned early in the tech cycle due to their “scale to develop AI and then roll AI across their platforms.”

As economic bottlenecks shift, he noted that the most successful investments will be in areas “capturing more value over time,” creating a broadening effect as the market matures.

Looking ahead, Wang offered an optimistic view on AI’s potential, noting that agentic AI “never needs to sleep, is on 24/7,” and can provide immediate, informed responses to customers.

His ultimate takeaway for investors: there has “never been a better time to be a tech investor.”

data.title: Verizon outlines $5B OpEx savings and targets up to 1M postpaid net adds in 2026 amid Frontier integration
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Daniel Schulman highlighted the company's transformation efforts, stating, "Our transformation will be driven by bold and meaningful actions to affect what is essentially a turnaround story." Schulman emphasized the organizational overhaul, including rightsizing, headcount reductions, and eliminating redundancies: "We are building an in-year war chest of $5 billion in OpEx savings, with a substantial portion realized by headcount reductions, alongside marketing efficiencies, real estate rationalization, contract renegotiations and more."
  • Schulman announced the closing of the Frontier acquisition, taking Verizon to over 30 million fiber passings and noting plans to add at least 2 million fiber passings this year, with a medium-term goal of reaching 40 million to 50 million. He stated, "We now expect to realize over $1 billion of run rate operating cost synergies by 2028, double our initial estimate."
  • Schulman reported a renewed MVNO partnership with Comcast and Charter, describing it as "an accretive deal that ensures their customers remain on the best network."
  • Verizon is preparing to launch a new value proposition in the first half of 2026 and aims to become "an AI-first company, deploying AI at scale."
  • CFO Anthony Skiadas said, "We finished 2025 with strong operational momentum while also achieving our full year financial guidance... In the fourth quarter, we added over 1 million net adds across mobility and broadband, our highest reported quarterly volumes in 6 years."

Outlook

  • Management guided for approximately 750,000 to 1 million postpaid phone net adds in 2026, which is 2 to 3 times the 2025 total. Schulman called the target "very doable... in year 1 of our transition."
  • 2026 mobility and broadband service revenue is expected to grow 2% to 3%, equating to approximately $93 billion. Wireless service revenue is expected to be approximately flat in 2026.
  • Adjusted EPS is projected in the range of $4.90 to $4.95 for the full year, representing 4% to 5% year-over-year growth. Free cash flow is expected to reach $21.5 billion or more, growing about 7% or more.
  • CapEx guidance for 2026 is $16 billion to $16.5 billion, a $4 billion reduction from combined 2025 expenditures of Verizon and Frontier.
  • The company plans at least $3 billion in share repurchases as part of a $25 billion authorization over three years.

Financial Results

  • In Q4, Verizon reported 616,000 postpaid phone net adds and 372,000 broadband net adds, with fixed wireless access contributing 319,000 and Fios Internet 67,000.
  • Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $11.9 billion for the quarter; full year adjusted EBITDA reached $50 billion, up $1.2 billion or 2.5% year-over-year.
  • Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $1.09, and full year adjusted EPS was $4.71, a 2.6% increase from the prior year.
  • Cash flow from operating activities was $37.1 billion for 2025, CapEx totaled $17 billion, and free cash flow reached $20.1 billion.
  • Net unsecured debt ended at $110.1 billion, a $3.6 billion year-over-year improvement; net unsecured debt to consolidated adjusted EBITDA was at 2.2x.
  • Verizon paid down approximately $5.7 billion of Frontier's debt since closing the acquisition and made $1.3 billion in discretionary pension contributions.

Q&A

  • Michael Ng, Goldman Sachs: Asked about investments to drive subscriber growth and whether improvements would come from churn reduction or marketing. Schulman responded, "If we reduce churn by 5 bps, we are already halfway to our target... we're going to leverage strongly all the convergence opportunities we have."
  • Ng, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about the increased fiber passings target. Schulman explained, "We want to get to at least 40 million or 50 million fiber passings... combination of what we're seeing at Frontier and what we can do internally here at Verizon."
  • Benjamin Swinburne, Morgan Stanley: Questioned customer lifetime values and CapEx reductions. Schulman stated the quality of new adds was high and emphasized churn reduction. Skiadas detailed that CapEx focus is now on mobility and broadband, with noncore areas being reduced or eliminated.
  • John Hodulik, UBS: Asked about composition of service revenue and broadband volume expectations. Skiadas said, "We guided to 2% to 3% mobility and broadband service revenue growth... we have now combined 16 million, over 16 million broadband subs in the base."
  • Michael Rollins, Citigroup: Queried on opportunities for ARPU growth and post-2026 cost savings. Schulman outlined "three waves of efficiency"—organizational, complexity reduction, and automation—and noted, "I think some of the broadband stuff that we're doing, where you're putting gig plus out there, that's real value to customers."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for specifics on growth drivers, churn, value proposition, and capital allocation, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarity on the execution of transformation and new targets.
  • Management maintained a confident and assertive tone, frequently emphasizing efficiency, execution, and the achievability of their targets. Schulman repeatedly underscored commitment: "We are heads down focused and ready to show the world that a fiscally responsible Verizon is playing to win."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there is a shift from planning and strategic intention to detailed execution, with management projecting greater confidence and urgency. Analysts' tone has shifted from cautious inquiry to more targeted questioning about operational specifics.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Previous quarter focused on strategic resets and foundational changes, whereas this quarter emphasized execution, cost reductions, and aggressive targets.
  • Guidance language shifted from broad ambitions to concrete metrics and specific volume and profitability targets for 2026.
  • Analysts in both quarters focused on churn, subscriber growth, and capital allocation, but this quarter included more questions about the impact and achievability of stated cost and revenue goals.
  • Management's tone evolved from outlining vision to communicating progress and measurable outcomes, displaying increased confidence in turnaround efforts and in confronting past underperformance.
  • Key metrics such as postpaid phone net adds and broadband net adds showed marked improvement, with explicit targets for further acceleration provided this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Schulman acknowledged the recent network outage and its negative impact on the brand, stating, "We did not meet the standard of excellence our customers expect and that we expect of ourselves."
  • Churn remains a pivotal risk, with elevated postpaid phone churn cited as a major challenge due to past pricing actions and competition.
  • Management identified ongoing promo amortization and lapping of prior year price increases as headwinds for 2026 revenue growth.
  • Execution risk around integrating Frontier and realizing projected synergies was mentioned, but management indicated confidence in the ongoing process and in achieving cost targets.
  • Analysts also questioned the sustainability of lifetime value and the ability to balance growth with efficiency without resorting to heavy promotional activity.

Final Takeaway

Verizon management underscored a transition from strategic planning to execution, highlighting robust Q4 results, a major cost reduction initiative, and ambitious 2026 targets for net adds, EPS, and free cash flow. With the integration of Frontier, renewed partnerships, and a sharpened focus on efficiency and customer experience, the company articulated a turnaround plan that it believes will drive both growth and shareholder returns, while closely managing operational and competitive risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Citizens cuts SAP SE to Market Perform on slow cloud backlog growth
data.description:
Brokerage firm Citizens downgraded SAP SE (SAP) to Market Perform from Market Outperform after the software firm reported disappointing top-line results in the fourth quarter and its cloud backlog growth missed market expectations.

The brokerage firm also lowered its 2026 non-IFRS EPS estimate to €6.84 from €6.87 (consensus €7.15) on total revenue of €39.9B and lowered its 2027 non-IFRS EPS estimate to €7.41 from €8.00 (consensus €8.48) on total revenue of €43.4B.  It also introduced its 2028 non-IFRS EPS estimate of €8.08 on total revenue of €47.5B.

Citizens’ analyst Patrick Walravens highlighted that current cloud backlog ((CRB)) growth of 25%, versus expectations of 26%, represents the slowest growth rate in nine quarters, as SAP experienced a more pronounced slowdown than anticipated due to a deal mix weighted toward larger transformations, many of which involve longer ramp periods or flexible structuring.

Walravens added that mounting geopolitical tensions have led many customers to place greater emphasis on exploring sovereign SaaS options.

SAP SE reported Q4 non-GAAP EPS of €1.62, beating estimates by €0.11. However, its Q4 revenue of €9.68B was €80M below market estimates.

data.title: Can Deckers Outdoor sustain momentum? Analysts split on growth potential
data.description:
Underpinned by record revenue and profits, and an outlook for FY26 that was better than Wall Street anticipated, Deckers Outdoor (DECK) continues to gain ground on Friday, jumping 13% at the open to reach a four-month high.

With a portfolio of footwear that captures two diverse segments of the market – HOKA for runners and UGG for a fashion-focused demographic – Deckers’ (DECK) healthy fiscal third quarter results led the company to raise its full year outlook.

While the results impressed investors, Wall Street analysts are split on the company’s future, with some raising doubts concerning UGGs’ enduring popularity and HOKA’s moat in the running segment.

Despite the outsized gains in the stock on Friday, Piper Sandler’s Anna Adreeva held onto her Underweight rating to reflect UGGs healthy but mature growth, decelerating forward demand, and the use of promotions to clear out seasonal carryover.

“We question what a mature portfolio of brands with record high EBIT margins should trade at,” Andreeva asks.

“Solid Q3 beat (including the inflection of UGG DTC) is driving the stock up but bears likely remain focused on a lack of U.S. growth, core gross margin pressure, and growing competitive headwinds in the athletic space,” says Wells Fargo analyst Ike Boruchow.

Boruchow worries that HOKA sales could slow as competitors encroach on the sneaker’s aesthetics and that UGG sales will suffer from a “notable slowdown in wholesale growth.”

The bulls, however, are encouraged by HOKA’s distribution potential with only about half of the targeted sporting goods doors represented, and DTC growth in UGGs.

“We think DECK is one of the highest-quality companies in our coverage, with an impressive multi-year track record of performance, two of the strongest brands in the industry, a strong management team, and a fortress balance sheet,” Needham’s Tom Nikic writes in his note to clients.

Accordingly, Nikic hikes his FY 26 and FY27 EPS outlook for the company by 7%-8% to $6.85 and $7.48, respectively, and his price target by 20% to $138.

Stifel’s Peter McGoldrick also values Deckers’ (DECK) diversified brands, strong balance sheet, and margin durability, and reaffirms his Buy rating with a raised target price of $140.

“We see FY26 guidance as a low bar,” Goldrick says, and argues that the inflection in DTC sales “validates high-quality consumer engagement and helps dispel a narrative of portfolio deterioration.”  

For the time being, Deckers’ (DECK) solid FQ3 results and FY26 guidance shifts the burden of proof on the bears. But over the coming quarters, Deckers (DECK) needs to prove the durability of its brands in a more competitive footwear landscape, DTC traction for UGGs, and whether it can maintain its wholesale durability to justify its current valuation, setting the stage for a closely watched 2026.

Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha authors are predominately bullish on Deckers Outdoor (DECK), but Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating  is more cautious, viewing the stock as a Hold with a Quant score of 2.74 giving low marks for growth.

data.title: Ioneer plunges after launching $50M capital raise
data.description:
Ioneer (IONR) -19.3% in early trading Friday after saying it secured firm commitments to raise ~$50M through a single-tranche placement to advance the development of its Rhyolite Ridge lithium and boron project in Nevada.

The company said it will issue ~400M new fully paid ordinary shares at A$0.18/share (~US$0.13/share).

Ioneer (IONR) said it plans to use the funds to advance its development plans, including advancing construction preparations and engaging with potential strategic partners for the Rhyolite Ridge project.

data.title: Franklin Resources Q1 profit exceeds consensus on net inflows
data.description:
Asset management giant Franklin Resources (BEN) on Friday turned in above-consensus earnings and revenue for Q1 of fiscal-year 2026, partly reflecting a return to quarterly long-term net inflows.

Q1 adjusted EPS of $0.70, topping the $0.55 average analyst estimate, rose from $0.67 in Q4 and $0.59 in last year's Q1.

Total operating revenue of $2.33B for the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2025, vs. $1.96B consensus, slipped from $2.34B in the quarter ended Sept. 30, 2025, and advanced from $2.25B in the quarter ended Dec. 31, 2024.

BEN shares gained 1.8% in late morning trading.

Q1 operating expenses fell to $2.05B from $2.26B in the earlier quarter and inched up from $2.03B a year before.

Long-term net inflows totaled $28.0B in Q1, up from the $11.9B of net outflows in Q4 and -$50.0B in the year-ago period.

"Excluding Western Asset Management, long-term net inflows totaled $34.6 billion, nearly double the prior year quarter, extending our track record to a ninth consecutive quarter of positive flows on a comparable basis," said Franklin Resources CEO Jenny Johnson.

Assets under management were $1.68T at the end of Q1, up 1% sequentially and +7% Y/Y.
